agent_output:
This request has critical business and regulatory implications that the engineering team has fundamentally mischaracterized. Let me produce my analysis.

{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a material change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and business capabilities.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management — intersects with Compliance, Billing, Customer Service, and Partner Integration bounded contexts",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete data artifact for a delivery: consignment details, proof of delivery, timestamps, signatures, chain-of-custody events, and associated financial data (invoices, customs declarations). This is a legal document, not just operational data." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (PoD)", "definition": "Evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient — often required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and tax/customs audits. Legally significant." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The business and legal rules governing how long records must be kept. Driven by the MOST restrictive applicable regulation, not by storage cost optimization." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The downstream use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory obligations (VAT filings, customs compliance, audit trails). Deleting upstream data can cause THEIR compliance failures." },
      { "term": "Right of Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)", "definition": "A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data — but explicitly does NOT apply where retention is necessary for legal compliance obligations (Art. 17(3)(b))." },
      { "term": "Consignment", "definition": "A shipment or batch of goods entrusted to a carrier for delivery, forming the core operational unit in logistics." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records that constitute tax/VAT documentation MUST be retained for the minimum period required by the applicable tax authority. In the UK (HMRC) this is 6 years. In most EU member states this is 7-10 years.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal obligation, not a business policy. Violation exposes the company to fines, failed audits, and potential criminal liability. No VP can override tax law.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record containing VAT-relevant data is deleted before the expiry of the applicable tax retention period for the jurisdiction of that delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Customs documentation for cross-border deliveries (EU/UK post-Brexit) must be retained for a minimum of 3 years (EU Customs Code) and up to 6 years (UK HMRC for customs records).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "EU Union Customs Code Art. 51 and UK customs legislation mandate retention. Destruction of customs records can trigger penalties and loss of authorized economic operator status.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record associated with a cross-border shipment is deleted before the applicable customs retention period expires."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery records must be available for the duration of any applicable limitation period for contractual or tortious claims related to that delivery. In the UK this is 6 years (Limitation Act 1980); in most EU jurisdictions 3-10 years.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without PoD, the company cannot defend against claims of non-delivery, misdelivery, or damage. Destroying evidence within the limitation period is a material legal risk.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Proof of Delivery record is deleted before the longest applicable limitation period for claims in the delivery's jurisdiction has expired."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Delivery data used by B2B customers for their own compliance reporting MUST remain accessible for at least the duration contractually agreed in the B2B service agreement, or the customer's own regulatory retention requirement — whichever is longer.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual obligation that could theoretically be renegotiated, but unilateral deletion without customer consent would constitute breach of contract.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted while an active B2B customer contract specifies access to that data for compliance reporting purposes."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "GDPR data minimization (Art. 5(1)(e)) requires that personal data is not kept longer than necessary — but 'necessary' is defined by the LONGEST applicable legal retention obligation, not the shortest convenient one.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR itself carves out exceptions for legal obligations. The correct GDPR-compliant approach is to retain for as long as legally required, then delete. 90 days is too short; 7 years may be correct for the regulated subset.",
      "testable_assertion": "Personal data within delivery records is retained for exactly the duration of the longest applicable legal retention obligation, then purged."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer is audited by their national tax authority 4 years after a delivery and requests the original delivery records for VAT reclaim evidence.", "expected_behavior": "Records must be available. Under the 90-day policy, they would have been destroyed 3+ years prior, causing the customer's audit to fail and exposing the platform to breach-of-contract claims." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer disputes a delivery 8 months after it occurred, claiming goods were never received.", "expected_behavior": "Proof of Delivery must be available to resolve the dispute. Under the 90-day policy, the PoD would be destroyed, leaving the platform unable to defend against the claim." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC conducts a VAT inspection and requests delivery records for the past 4 years.", "expected_behavior": "Complete records must be produced. Failure to do so can result in estimated (higher) tax assessments and penalties." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border shipment between UK and EU is flagged for customs audit 2 years after delivery.", "expected_behavior": "Customs documentation must be available. Destruction after 90 days would constitute failure to comply with customs record-keeping obligations." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer's contract stipulates 5-year data access for their own ISO/compliance certifications.", "expected_behavior": "Data must remain accessible per contract terms. Unilateral deletion is breach of contract regardless of internal storage cost considerations." },
    { "scenario": "Insurance claim for damaged goods filed 6 months after delivery.", "expected_behavior": "Delivery chain-of-custody records, condition-at-delivery evidence, and PoD must be available for claim processing. 90-day deletion destroys this evidence." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Regulatory fines: GDPR fines up to 4% of global annual turnover for non-compliant data practices, tax authority penalties for missing records, customs penalties for destroyed documentation. (2) Lost disputes: inability to defend against non-delivery claims means automatic loss — could be millions annually depending on volume. (3) B2B customer churn: logistics companies that cannot use the platform for compliance will leave. (4) Insurance: inability to produce delivery evidence invalidates claims. The storage cost savings are trivial compared to any ONE of these risks materializing.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL DISRUPTION. Customer service loses ability to investigate any delivery issue older than 90 days. Finance loses audit trail for reconciliation. Legal loses evidence for dispute resolution. B2B integration partners lose the data feed they depend on. The 7-year policy exists because it is the ceiling of overlapping legal requirements across EU/UK jurisdictions.",
    "user": "B2C consumers lose the ability to raise disputes or track historical deliveries after 90 days. B2B customers face compliance failures in their own organizations when upstream data disappears. Trust in the platform is fundamentally undermined.",
    "risk": "EXISTENTIAL for EU/UK operations. This change, if implemented, creates simultaneous exposure to: tax authority penalties, customs violations, breach of B2B contracts, inability to defend consumer disputes, potential GDPR enforcement action (paradoxically — because the deletion policy would NOT be lawful under GDPR when legal retention obligations exist), and loss of authorized economic operator status for customs.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B logistics customers", "impact": "Cannot fulfill their own compliance/audit obligations. Will likely churn or pursue breach-of-contract claims. This is a revenue-threatening impact." },
      { "group": "B2C consumers", "impact": "Lose ability to dispute deliveries, request refunds, or file insurance claims after 90 days. Consumer protection risk." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax team", "impact": "Cannot produce records for VAT audits. Exposed to estimated tax assessments and penalties." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance team", "impact": "Cannot defend against claims, cannot produce evidence for regulatory inquiries. Professional liability risk." },
      { "group": "Customer Service", "impact": "Cannot investigate or resolve any delivery issue older than 90 days. Service quality collapse." },
      { "group": "Insurance partners", "impact": "Claims become unprocessable without supporting delivery documentation." },
      { "group": "Customs authorities (EU/UK)", "impact": "Platform fails record-keeping obligations. Risk of losing preferential customs status." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 92,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for delivery record retention in EU/UK is well-established and unambiguous. Tax retention requirements (6-7+ years), customs documentation requirements (3-6 years), and contractual limitation periods (6 years UK) are matters of settled law, not interpretation. The only uncertainty is the exact retention period per EU member state, which varies but ALL exceed 90 days by years. The characterization of this change as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC 6-year retention requirement for VAT records",
      "EU customs documentation retention obligations",
      "Breach of B2B contracts if data deleted unilaterally",
      "Loss of dispute resolution capability",
      "The 90-day period is orders of magnitude too short for any regulated jurisdiction"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention period per individual EU member state (varies from 5-10 years)",
      "Specific terms of existing B2B contracts (not reviewed)",
      "Whether the platform has already segregated regulated data from operational data"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery records contain VAT-relevant financial data (standard in logistics)",
      "The platform handles cross-border EU/UK shipments post-Brexit",
      "B2B contracts include data access provisions for compliance purposes",
      "The current 7-year policy was set with legal counsel input and reflects the regulatory ceiling"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The engineering team characterizes this as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes.' This is fundamentally incorrect. Deleting legally-required records is not an infrastructure concern — it is a compliance violation. The framing must be corrected before any further work proceeds. The team may not understand that data retention IS business logic in regulated industries.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Finance", "B2B customers", "B2C consumers", "Executive leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and retain the 7-year policy",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive regulated data (PoD, tax, customs) for 7 years in cold storage, delete purely operational/telemetry data after 90 days",
        "C: Engage legal counsel to define per-jurisdiction minimum retention periods, then implement the shortest compliant period per data category"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C is the correct long-term approach, with Option B as an acceptable interim measure. Option A (status quo) is safe but doesn't address the legitimate storage cost concern. Under NO circumstances should Option 'delete everything at 90 days' proceed. This proposal must be escalated to Legal and the DPO before any implementation begins."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion of delivery records at 90 days destroys tax/VAT documentation required by HMRC (6 years) and EU member state tax authorities (5-10 years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys customs documentation required under EU Union Customs Code and UK customs legislation (3-6 years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys Proof of Delivery evidence within the statutory limitation period for claims (6 years UK, 3-10 years EU)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days breaches B2B contractual obligations for data access and compliance reporting", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Blanket 90-day deletion is not GDPR-compliant because it ignores the legal basis for longer retention under Art. 17(3)(b) — creating a paradoxical situation where the 'privacy-friendly' deletion actually violates GDPR", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" }
  ]
}
